description: The provided image pixels display a publisher user-interface element labeled Check for updates containing a bookmark icon and colored circular graphics, which contains no discernible scientific data, micrographs, spectra, or plots. Consequently, all material-specific visual information such as phase distributions, microstructural morphologies, diffraction peaks, or mechanical curves is completely unreadable from the figure itself. Based on the broader paper context, the research concerns bi-directional ferrite-austenite transformation in an Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel during warm compression at 500 °C and 600 °C under a strain rate of 0.001 s-1, employing EBSD phase mapping, TEM bright-field imaging and selected-area electron diffraction, SEM-EDS compositional analysis, and true-stress-strain measurements. However, because the current image lacks any visible axes, legends, scale bars, sample identifiers, or experimental data panels, the exact role of this specific figure-whether it represents a micrograph, a diffraction pattern, a spectroscopy plot, or a mechanical curve-cannot be visually confirmed, and the detailed findings reported in the text (including deformation bands, dislocation cells, and phase partitioning) are not directly observable here.

Figure 1 presents the initial microstructure and compressive mechanical behavior of an Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel annealed at 950 °C for 1 h. Subfigure (a) is an EBSD phase map revealing a triplex microstructure of ferrite (green), martensite (yellow), and austenite (red). Subfigure (b) is a TEM bright-field micrograph with corresponding SAED patterns confirming the γ-austenite, α-ferrite, and α′-martensite phases; white arrows indicate initial ferrite–ferrite boundaries. Subfigures (c) and (d) display the true-stress–strain curves at 500 and 600 °C (0.001 s⁻¹) and the extracted maximum flow stress and 0.2 % yield stress versus temperature from 100 to 700 °C, respectively. The flow stress remains mechanically stable between 200 and 500 °C but drops significantly from 500 to 600 °C, as highlighted by the blue-dashed oval in (d). Appreciable flow softening after a true strain of ~0.3 at 500–600 °C is observed, which the authors attribute to strain-induced transformations rather than dynamic recrystallization. Together, these data establish the starting triplex condition and the temperature-dependent deformation response that underpins the subsequent analysis of bidirectional ferrite–austenite transformation during warm compression.

This figure presents EBSD phase maps overlaid on image quality maps and corresponding separated phase maps of an Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel after warm compression. Subfigures (a) and (b) show the PM/IQ overlays for samples compressed at 500 °C and 600 °C, respectively, under a strain rate of 0.001 s−1 to a true strain of 0.6, where green indicates BCC and red indicates FCC. Subfigures (c) and (d) display the corresponding three-phase maps, with green, yellow, and red representing ferrite, martensite, and austenite, respectively. The 500 °C condition exhibits frequently distributed deformation bands throughout the microstructure, a feature not observed at 600 °C due to greater strain distribution capability at higher temperature. Both deformation conditions retain a triplex microstructure. These maps provide direct microstructural evidence that strain localization along deformation bands at the lower deformation temperature promotes simultaneous ferrite-to-austenite and austenite-to-ferrite transformations, thereby supporting the proposed bi-directional transformation mechanism.

Fig. 3 presents a multimicroscopy characterization of deformed Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel under warm compression. Panels (a) and (b) are TEM bright-field images and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns obtained at 500 °C and an interrupted strain of 0.3. Panel (a) reveals tangled dislocations, high-dislocation-density walls (HDDWs), and intersecting {110} and {112} slip traces within ferrite, indicating multiple slip-system activation. Panel (b) shows fresh ferrite grains forming within prior austenite islands (white arrows), corroborated by SAED patterns indexed as ferrite (Z=[113]) and austenite (Z=[111]). Panels (c) and (d) (higher-magnification views of selected regions from Fig. 2, per caption) display banded structures at 500 °C and true strain 0.6, where red and green arrows denote austenite and ferrite formation, respectively, along deformation bands—evidence of simultaneous deformation-induced austenite-to-ferrite and ferrite-to-austenite transformations. Panel (e) shows the microstructure after deformation at 600 °C, where such transformation bands are absent. Together, the images demonstrate that low-temperature (500 °C) deformation promotes slip-band intersection and HDDWs in ferrite, strain-induced ferrite nucleation in austenite, and bidirectional phase transformation as a strain-compensation mechanism causing flow softening.

Figure 4 presents Thermo-Calc® thermodynamic modeling results for the Fe-11.15Mn-5.6Al-0.07C low-density steel. Subfigures (a) and (b) display the equilibrium mass percentages of Mn and Al in constituent phases (BCC_A2 ferrite, FCC_A1 austenite, and FCC_A1#2) as a function of temperature, highlighting that Mn partitions strongly into austenite while Al enriches in ferrite at the annealed temperature near 950 °C. Subfigures (c) and (e) plot the Gibbs free energy of BCC and FCC phases versus Mn content at 500 °C and 550 °C, respectively, whereas (d) and (f) plot the same versus Al content at these temperatures. In each plot, point A marks the composition where both phases possess equal Gibbs free energy, while points B and C indicate the actual FCC and BCC compositions after elemental partitioning extracted from (a), (b) and Table 1. The data reveal that when partitioned compositions are considered, the Gibbs free energies of ferrite and austenite become nearly identical at 500 °C, providing the thermodynamic basis for simultaneous deformation-induced ferrite-to-austenite and austenite-to-ferrite transformations. In contrast, varying Al content exerts minimal influence on phase stability, indicating that Mn partitioning primarily governs the phase equilibrium.
